obj.title: Timur Gareyev – blindfold chess champion
obj.content:
[br/]
[p:a]Next month, a chess player named Timur Gareyev will take on nearly 50 opponents at once. But that is not the hard part. While his challengers will play the games as normal, Gareyev himself will be blindfolded. Even by world record standards, it sets a high bar for human performance. The 28-year-old already stands out in the rarefied world of blindfold chess. He has a fondness for bright clothes and unusual hairstyles, and he gets his kicks from the adventure sport of BASE jumping. He has already proved himself a strong chess player, too. In a 10-hour chess marathon in 2013, Gareyev played 33 games in his head simultaneously. He won 29 and lost none. The skill has become his brand: he calls himself the Blindfold King. [br/][br/]
[p:b]But Gareyev's prowess has drawn interest from beyond the chess-playing community. In the hope of understanding how he and others like him can perform such mental feats, researchers at the University of California in Los Angeles (UCLA) called him in for tests. They now have their first results. 'The ability to play a game of chess with your eyes closed is not a far reach for most accomplished players,' said Jesse Rissman, who runs a memory lab at UCLA. 'But the thing that's so remarkable about Timur and a few other individuals is the number of games they can keep active at once. To me it is simply astonishing.' [br/][br/]
[p:c]Gareyev learned to play chess in his native Uzbekistan when he was six years old. Tutored by his grandfather, he entered his first tournament aged eight and soon became obsessed with competitions. At 16, he was crowned Asia's youngest ever chess grandmaster. He moved to the US soon after, and as a student helped his university win its first national chess championship. In 2013, Gareyev was ranked the third best chess player in the US. [br/][br/]
[p:d]To the uninitiated, blindfold chess seems to call for superhuman skill. But displays of the feat go back centuries. The first recorded game in Europe was played in 13th-century Florence. In 1947, the Argentinian grandmaster Miguel Najdorf played 45 simultaneous games in his mind, winning 39 in the 24-hour session. [br/][br/]
[p:e]Accomplished players can develop the skill of playing blind even without realising it. The nature of the game is to run through possible moves in the mind to see how they play out. From this, regular players develop a memory for the patterns the pieces make, the defences and attacks. 'You recreate it in your mind,' said Gareyev. 'A lot of players are capable of doing what I'm doing.' The real mental challenge comes from playing multiple games at once in the head. Not only must the positions of each piece on every board be memorised, they must be recalled faithfully when needed, updated with each player's moves, and then reliably stored again, so the brain can move on to the next board. First moves can be tough to remember because they are fairly uninteresting. But the ends of games are taxing too, as exhaustion sets in. When Gareyev is tired, his recall can get patchy. He sometimes makes moves based on only a fragmented memory of the pieces' positions. [br/][br/]
[p:f]The scientists first had Gareyev perform some standard memory tests. These assessed his ability to hold numbers, pictures and words in mind. One classic test measures how many numbers a person can repeat, both forwards and backwards, soon after hearing them. Most people manage about seven. 'He was not exceptional on any of these standard tests,' said Rissman. 'We didn't find anything other than playing chess that he seems to be supremely gifted at.' But next came the brain scans. With Gareyev lying down in the machine, Rissman looked at how well connected the various regions of the chess player's brain were. Though the results are tentative and as yet unpublished, the scans found much greater than average communication between parts of Gareyev's brain that make up what is called the frontoparietal control network. Of 63 people scanned alongside the chess player, only one or two scored more highly on the measure. 'You use this network in almost any complex task. It helps you to allocate attention, keep rules in mind, and work out whether you should be responding or not,' said Rissman. [br/][br/]
[p:g]It was not the only hint of something special in Gareyev's brain. The scans also suggest that Gareyev's visual network is more highly connected to other brain parts than usual. Initial results suggest that the areas of his brain that process visual images – such as chess boards – may have stronger links to other brain regions, and so be more powerful than normal. While the analyses are not finalised yet, they may hold the first clues to Gareyev's extraordinary ability. [br/][br/]
[p:h]For the world record attempt, Gareyev hopes to play 47 blindfold games at once in about 16 hours. He will need to win 80% to claim the title. 'I don't worry too much about the winning percentage, that's never been an issue for me,' he said. 'The most important part of blindfold chess for me is that I have found the one thing that I can fully dedicate myself to. I miss having an obsession.'
obj.content_zh:
[br/] 
[b]A.[/b]下个月，一位名叫铁木尔-加列耶夫的棋手将同时迎战近 50 名对手。但这还不是最难的。他的挑战者将正常进行比赛，而加列耶夫本人将被蒙上眼睛。即使按照世界纪录的标准，这也为人类的表现设定了很高的标准。现年 28 岁的加列耶夫已经在蒙眼国际象棋的稀有世界中脱颖而出。他喜欢鲜艳的衣服和不寻常的发型，并从 BASE 跳伞这项冒险运动中获得乐趣。他已经证明了自己也是一名强大的棋手。2013 年，在一场长达 10 小时的国际象棋马拉松比赛中，加列耶夫在头脑中同时下了 33 盘棋。他赢了 29 盘，一盘未输。这项技能已成为他的品牌：他称自己为 “蒙眼国王”。[br/]
[b]B.[/b]但是，加列耶夫的能力引起了国际象棋界以外的兴趣。加州大学洛杉矶分校（UCLA）的研究人员希望了解他和其他像他一样的人是如何完成这样的智力壮举的。现在他们有了第一批结果。加州大学洛杉矶分校记忆实验室负责人杰西-里斯曼（Jesse Rissman）说："对于大多数有成就的棋手来说，闭着眼睛下一盘棋的能力并不遥远。但铁木尔和其他一些人的非凡之处在于，他们能同时保持活跃的棋局数量。在我看来，这简直令人吃惊。[br/] [b]C.[/b]加列耶夫六岁时在家乡乌兹别克斯坦学习下棋。在祖父的指导下，他在八岁时第一次参加比赛，并很快迷上了比赛。16 岁时，他成为亚洲有史以来最年轻的国际象棋大师。不久后，他移居美国，并在学生时代帮助所在大学赢得了首个全国国际象棋锦标赛。2013 年，加列耶夫被评为美国最佳棋手第三名。[br/] 
[b]D.[/b]对不了解的人来说，蒙眼国际象棋似乎需要超人的技巧。但是，这种特技的展示可以追溯到几个世纪以前。欧洲有记载的第一盘棋是在 13 世纪的佛罗伦萨进行的。1947 年，阿根廷特级大师米格尔-纳伊多夫（Miguel Najdorf）在脑海中同时下了 45 盘棋，在 24 小时内赢了 39 盘。[br/]
[b]E.[/b]棋艺高超的棋手甚至会在不知不觉中掌握盲棋技巧。下棋的本质是在脑海中演算可能的棋步，看看它们是如何走出来的。由此，普通棋手会对棋子的布局、防御和攻击形成记忆。加列耶夫说："你会在脑海中重现棋局。很多球员都能做到我现在这样。真正的智力挑战来自于同时在头脑中进行多盘棋。不仅要记住每块棋盘上每颗棋子的位置，还必须在需要时忠实地回忆起这些位置，并根据每位棋手的棋步进行更新，然后再次可靠地存储起来，以便大脑能继续下一盘棋。第一步棋可能很难记住，因为它们相当无趣。但棋局结束时也会让人疲惫不堪。当加列耶夫疲惫时，他的记忆会变得模糊不清。有时，他只能凭借对棋子位置的零碎记忆下棋。[br/]
[b]F.[/b]科学家们首先让加列耶夫进行了一些标准的记忆测试。这些测试评估了他记忆数字、图片和文字的能力。其中一个经典测试是测量一个人在听到数字后能正反重复多少个数字。大多数人大约能复述七个数字。里斯曼说："他在这些标准测试中都没有出类拔萃的表现。'除了下棋，我们没有发现他似乎在其他方面有超常的天赋。'但接下来是脑部扫描。加列耶夫躺在机器里，里斯曼查看了棋手大脑各区域的连接情况。虽然结果还只是初步的，尚未公布，但扫描发现，加列耶夫大脑各部分之间的交流远远超过了平均水平，而这些部分构成了所谓的前顶叶控制网络。在与加列耶夫同时接受扫描的 63 人中，只有一两个人的得分更高。在几乎所有复杂的任务中，你都会用到这个网络。它能帮助你分配注意力，牢记规则，并确定是否应该做出反应，"里斯曼说。[br/]
[b]G.[/b]这并不是加列耶夫大脑中唯一的特殊暗示。扫描结果还表明，加列耶夫的视觉网络与其他大脑区域的联系比平常更加紧密。初步结果表明，他大脑中处理视觉图像（如棋盘）的区域可能与其他脑区有更强的联系，因此比正常人更强大。虽然分析结果尚未最终确定，但它们可能是加列耶夫非凡能力的第一批线索。[br/]
[b]H.[/b]为了打破世界纪录，加列耶夫希望在大约16小时内同时进行47次蒙眼游戏。他需要赢得 80% 的胜利才能夺冠。他说："我并不太在意胜率，这对我来说从来都不是问题。对我来说，蒙眼棋最重要的部分是我找到了一件可以全身心投入的事情。我很怀念这种痴迷的感觉。[br/]
obj.html_content:
aNext month, a chess player named Timur Gareyev will take on nearly 50 opponents at once. But that is not the hard part. While his challengers will play the games as normal, Gareyev himself will be blindfolded. Even by world record standards, it sets a high bar for human performance. The 28-year-old already stands out in the rarefied world of blindfold chess. He has a fondness for bright clothes and unusual hairstyles, and he gets his kicks from the adventure sport of BASE jumping. He has already proved himself a strong chess player, too. In a 10-hour chess marathon in 2013, Gareyev played 33 games in his head simultaneously. He won 29 and lost none. The skill has become his brand: he calls himself the Blindfold King. 



bBut Gareyev's prowess has drawn interest from beyond the chess-playing community. In the hope of understanding how he and others like him can perform such mental feats, researchers at the University of California in Los Angeles (UCLA) called him in for tests. They now have their first results. 'The ability to play a game of chess with your eyes closed is not a far reach for most accomplished players,' said Jesse Rissman, who runs a memory lab at UCLA. 'But the thing that's so remarkable about Timur and a few other individuals is the number of games they can keep active at once. To me it is simply astonishing.' 



cGareyev learned to play chess in his native Uzbekistan when he was six years old. Tutored by his grandfather, he entered his first tournament aged eight and soon became obsessed with competitions. At 16, he was crowned Asia's youngest ever chess grandmaster. He moved to the US soon after, and as a student helped his university win its first national chess championship. In 2013, Gareyev was ranked the third best chess player in the US. 



dTo the uninitiated, blindfold chess seems to call for superhuman skill. But displays of the feat go back centuries. The first recorded game in Europe was played in 13th-century Florence. In 1947, the Argentinian grandmaster Miguel Najdorf played 45 simultaneous games in his mind, winning 39 in the 24-hour session. 



eAccomplished players can develop the skill of playing blind even without realising it. The nature of the game is to run through possible moves in the mind to see how they play out. From this, regular players develop a memory for the patterns the pieces make, the defences and attacks. 'You recreate it in your mind,' said Gareyev. 'A lot of players are capable of doing what I'm doing.' The real mental challenge comes from playing multiple games at once in the head. Not only must the positions of each piece on every board be memorised, they must be recalled faithfully when needed, updated with each player's moves, and then reliably stored again, so the brain can move on to the next board. First moves can be tough to remember because they are fairly uninteresting. But the ends of games are taxing too, as exhaustion sets in. When Gareyev is tired, his recall can get patchy. He sometimes makes moves based on only a fragmented memory of the pieces' positions. 



fThe scientists first had Gareyev perform some standard memory tests. These assessed his ability to hold numbers, pictures and words in mind. One classic test measures how many numbers a person can repeat, both forwards and backwards, soon after hearing them. Most people manage about seven. 'He was not exceptional on any of these standard tests,' said Rissman. 'We didn't find anything other than playing chess that he seems to be supremely gifted at.' But next came the brain scans. With Gareyev lying down in the machine, Rissman looked at how well connected the various regions of the chess player's brain were. Though the results are tentative and as yet unpublished, the scans found much greater than average communication between parts of Gareyev's brain that make up what is called the frontoparietal control network. Of 63 people scanned alongside the chess player, only one or two scored more highly on the measure. 'You use this network in almost any complex task. It helps you to allocate attention, keep rules in mind, and work out whether you should be responding or not,' said Rissman. 



gIt was not the only hint of something special in Gareyev's brain. The scans also suggest that Gareyev's visual network is more highly connected to other brain parts than usual. Initial results suggest that the areas of his brain that process visual images – such as chess boards – may have stronger links to other brain regions, and so be more powerful than normal. While the analyses are not finalised yet, they may hold the first clues to Gareyev's extraordinary ability. 



hFor the world record attempt, Gareyev hopes to play 47 blindfold games at once in about 16 hours. He will need to win 80% to claim the title. 'I don't worry too much about the winning percentage, that's never been an issue for me,' he said. 'The most important part of blindfold chess for me is that I have found the one thing that I can fully dedicate myself to. I miss having an obsession.'

obj.content_zh:
[br/] 
[b]A.[/b]下个月，一位名叫铁木尔-加列耶夫的棋手将同时迎战近 50 名对手。但这还不是最难的。他的挑战者将正常进行比赛，而加列耶夫本人将被蒙上眼睛。即使按照世界纪录的标准，这也为人类的表现设定了很高的标准。现年 28 岁的加列耶夫已经在蒙眼国际象棋的稀有世界中脱颖而出。他喜欢鲜艳的衣服和不寻常的发型，并从 BASE 跳伞这项冒险运动中获得乐趣。他已经证明了自己也是一名强大的棋手。2013 年，在一场长达 10 小时的国际象棋马拉松比赛中，加列耶夫在头脑中同时下了 33 盘棋。他赢了 29 盘，一盘未输。这项技能已成为他的品牌：他称自己为 “蒙眼国王”。[br/]

[b]E.[/b]棋艺高超的棋手甚至会在不知不觉中掌握盲棋技巧。下棋的本质是在脑海中演算可能的棋步，看看它们是如何走出来的。由此，普通棋手会对棋子的布局、防御和攻击形成记忆。加列耶夫说："你会在脑海中重现棋局。很多球员都能做到我现在这样。真正的智力挑战来自于同时在头脑中进行多盘棋。不仅要记住每块棋盘上每颗棋子的位置，还必须在需要时忠实地回忆起这些位置，并根据每位棋手的棋步进行更新，然后再次可靠地存储起来，以便大脑能继续下一盘棋。第一步棋可能很难记住，因为它们相当无趣。但棋局结束时也会让人疲惫不堪。当加列耶夫疲惫时，他的记忆会变得模糊不清。有时，他只能凭借对棋子位置的零碎记忆下棋。[br/]
[b]F.[/b]科学家们首先让加列耶夫进行了一些标准的记忆测试。这些测试评估了他记忆数字、图片和文字的能力。其中一个经典测试是测量一个人在听到数字后能正反重复多少个数字。大多数人大约能复述七个数字。里斯曼说："他在这些标准测试中都没有出类拔萃的表现。'除了下棋，我们没有发现他似乎在其他方面有超常的天赋。'但接下来是脑部扫描。加列耶夫躺在机器里，里斯曼查看了棋手大脑各区域的连接情况。虽然结果还只是初步的，尚未公布，但扫描发现，加列耶夫大脑各部分之间的交流远远超过了平均水平，而这些部分构成了所谓的前顶叶控制网络。在与加列耶夫同时接受扫描的 63 人中，只有一两个人的得分更高。在几乎所有复杂的任务中，你都会用到这个网络。它能帮助你分配注意力，牢记规则，并确定是否应该做出反应，"里斯曼说。[br/]
[b]G.[/b]这并不是加列耶夫大脑中唯一的特殊暗示。扫描结果还表明，加列耶夫的视觉网络与其他大脑区域的联系比平常更加紧密。初步结果表明，他大脑中处理视觉图像（如棋盘）的区域可能与其他脑区有更强的联系，因此比正常人更强大。虽然分析结果尚未最终确定，但它们可能是加列耶夫非凡能力的第一批线索。[br/]
[b]H.[/b]为了打破世界纪录，加列耶夫希望在大约16小时内同时进行47次蒙眼游戏。他需要赢得 80% 的胜利才能夺冠。他说："我并不太在意胜率，这对我来说从来都不是问题。对我来说，蒙眼棋最重要的部分是我找到了一件可以全身心投入的事情。我很怀念这种痴迷的感觉。[br/]
obj.html_content:
aNext month, a chess player named Timur Gareyev will take on nearly 50 opponents at once. But that is not the hard part. While his challengers will play the games as normal, Gareyev himself will be blindfolded. Even by world record standards, it sets a high bar for human performance. The 28-year-old already stands out in the rarefied world of blindfold chess. He has a fondness for bright clothes and unusual hairstyles, and he gets his kicks from the adventure sport of BASE jumping. He has already proved himself a strong chess player, too. In a 10-hour chess marathon in 2013, Gareyev played 33 games in his head simultaneously. He won 29 and lost none. The skill has become his brand: he calls himself the Blindfold King. 



bBut Gareyev's prowess has drawn interest from beyond the chess-playing community. In the hope of understanding how he and others like him can perform such mental feats, researchers at the University of California in Los Angeles (UCLA) called him in for tests. They now have their first results. 'The ability to play a game of chess with your eyes closed is not a far reach for most accomplished players,' said Jesse Rissman, who runs a memory lab at UCLA. 'But the thing that's so remarkable about Timur and a few other individuals is the number of games they can keep active at once. To me it is simply astonishing.' 



cGareyev learned to play chess in his native Uzbekistan when he was six years old. Tutored by his grandfather, he entered his first tournament aged eight and soon became obsessed with competitions. At 16, he was crowned Asia's youngest ever chess grandmaster. He moved to the US soon after, and as a student helped his university win its first national chess championship. In 2013, Gareyev was ranked the third best chess player in the US. 



dTo the uninitiated, blindfold chess seems to call for superhuman skill. But displays of the feat go back centuries. The first recorded game in Europe was played in 13th-century Florence. In 1947, the Argentinian grandmaster Miguel Najdorf played 45 simultaneous games in his mind, winning 39 in the 24-hour session. 



eAccomplished players can develop the skill of playing blind even without realising it. The nature of the game is to run through possible moves in the mind to see how they play out. From this, regular players develop a memory for the patterns the pieces make, the defences and attacks. 'You recreate it in your mind,' said Gareyev. 'A lot of players are capable of doing what I'm doing.' The real mental challenge comes from playing multiple games at once in the head. Not only must the positions of each piece on every board be memorised, they must be recalled faithfully when needed, updated with each player's moves, and then reliably stored again, so the brain can move on to the next board. First moves can be tough to remember because they are fairly uninteresting. But the ends of games are taxing too, as exhaustion sets in. When Gareyev is tired, his recall can get patchy. He sometimes makes moves based on only a fragmented memory of the pieces' positions. 



fThe scientists first had Gareyev perform some standard memory tests. These assessed his ability to hold numbers, pictures and words in mind. One classic test measures how many numbers a person can repeat, both forwards and backwards, soon after hearing them. Most people manage about seven. 'He was not exceptional on any of these standard tests,' said Rissman. 'We didn't find anything other than playing chess that he seems to be supremely gifted at.' But next came the brain scans. With Gareyev lying down in the machine, Rissman looked at how well connected the various regions of the chess player's brain were. Though the results are tentative and as yet unpublished, the scans found much greater than average communication between parts of Gareyev's brain that make up what is called the frontoparietal control network. Of 63 people scanned alongside the chess player, only one or two scored more highly on the measure. 'You use this network in almost any complex task. It helps you to allocate attention, keep rules in mind, and work out whether you should be responding or not,' said Rissman. 



gIt was not the only hint of something special in Gareyev's brain. The scans also suggest that Gareyev's visual network is more highly connected to other brain parts than usual. Initial results suggest that the areas of his brain that process visual images – such as chess boards – may have stronger links to other brain regions, and so be more powerful than normal. While the analyses are not finalised yet, they may hold the first clues to Gareyev's extraordinary ability. 



hFor the world record attempt, Gareyev hopes to play 47 blindfold games at once in about 16 hours. He will need to win 80% to claim the title. 'I don't worry too much about the winning percentage, that's never been an issue for me,' he said. 'The most important part of blindfold chess for me is that I have found the one thing that I can fully dedicate myself to. I miss having an obsession.'

obj.content:
[center][b]How the research was carried out[/b][/center][br/]
The researchers started by testing Gareyev's [blank]37[/blank]; for example, he was required to recall a string of [blank]38[/blank] in order and also in reverse order. Although his performance was normal, scans showed an unusual amount of [blank]39[/blank] within the areas of Gareyev's brain that are concerned with directing attention. In addition, the scans raised the possibility of unusual strength in the parts of his brain that deal with [blank]40[/blank] input.

In addition, the scans raised the possibility of unusual strength in the parts of his brain that deal with   input.

ONE WORD ONLY
from the passage for each answer.
Write the correct letter in boxes 37-40 on your answer sheet.

obj.title: Timur Gareyev – blindfold chess champion
obj.content:
[br/]
[p:a]Next month, a chess player named Timur Gareyev will take on nearly 50 opponents at once. But that is not the hard part. While his challengers will play the games as normal, Gareyev himself will be blindfolded. Even by world record standards, it sets a high bar for human performance. The 28-year-old already stands out in the rarefied world of blindfold chess. He has a fondness for bright clothes and unusual hairstyles, and he gets his kicks from the adventure sport of BASE jumping. He has already proved himself a strong chess player, too. In a 10-hour chess marathon in 2013, Gareyev played 33 games in his head simultaneously. He won 29 and lost none. The skill has become his brand: he calls himself the Blindfold King. [br/][br/]
[p:b]But Gareyev's prowess has drawn interest from beyond the chess-playing community. In the hope of understanding how he and others like him can perform such mental feats, researchers at the University of California in Los Angeles (UCLA) called him in for tests. They now have their first results. 'The ability to play a game of chess with your eyes closed is not a far reach for most accomplished players,' said Jesse Rissman, who runs a memory lab at UCLA. 'But the thing that's so remarkable about Timur and a few other individuals is the number of games they can keep active at once. To me it is simply astonishing.' [br/][br/]
[p:c]Gareyev learned to play chess in his native Uzbekistan when he was six years old. Tutored by his grandfather, he entered his first tournament aged eight and soon became obsessed with competitions. At 16, he was crowned Asia's youngest ever chess grandmaster. He moved to the US soon after, and as a student helped his university win its first national chess championship. In 2013, Gareyev was ranked the third best chess player in the US. [br/][br/]
[p:d]To the uninitiated, blindfold chess seems to call for superhuman skill. But displays of the feat go back centuries. The first recorded game in Europe was played in 13th-century Florence. In 1947, the Argentinian grandmaster Miguel Najdorf played 45 simultaneous games in his mind, winning 39 in the 24-hour session. [br/][br/]
[p:e]Accomplished players can develop the skill of playing blind even without realising it. The nature of the game is to run through possible moves in the mind to see how they play out. From this, regular players develop a memory for the patterns the pieces make, the defences and attacks. 'You recreate it in your mind,' said Gareyev. 'A lot of players are capable of doing what I'm doing.' The real mental challenge comes from playing multiple games at once in the head. First moves can be tough to remember because they are fairly uninteresting. But the ends of games are taxing too, as exhaustion sets in. When Gareyev is tired, his recall can get patchy. He sometimes makes moves based on only a fragmented memory of the pieces' positions. [br/][br/]
[p:f]The scientists first had Gareyev perform some standard memory tests. These assessed his ability to hold numbers, pictures and words in mind. One classic test measures how many numbers a person can repeat, both forwards and backwards, soon after hearing them. Most people manage about seven. With Gareyev lying down in the machine, Rissman looked at how well connected the various regions of the chess player's brain were. Though the results are tentative and as yet unpublished, the scans found much greater than average communication between parts of Gareyev's brain that make up what is called the frontoparietal control network. Of 63 people scanned alongside the chess player, only one or two scored more highly on the measure. 'You use this network in almost any complex task. It helps you to allocate attention, keep rules in mind, and work out whether you should be responding or not,' said Rissman. [br/][br/]
[p:g]It was not the only hint of something special in Gareyev's brain. The scans also suggest that Gareyev's visual network is more highly connected to other brain parts than usual. Initial results suggest that the areas of his brain that process visual images – such as chess boards – may have stronger links to other brain regions, and so be more powerful than normal. While the analyses are not finalised yet, they may hold the first clues to Gareyev's extraordinary ability. [br/][br/]
[p:h]For the world record attempt, Gareyev hopes to play 47 blindfold games at once in about 16 hours. He will need to win 80% to claim the title. 'I don't worry too much about the winning percentage, that's never been an issue for me,' he said. 'The most important part of blindfold chess for me is that I have found the one thing that I can fully dedicate myself to. I miss having an obsession.'
obj.content_zh:
[br/] 
[b]A.[/b]下个月，一位名叫铁木尔-加列耶夫的棋手将同时迎战近 50 名对手。但这还不是最难的。他的挑战者将正常进行比赛，而加列耶夫本人将被蒙上眼睛。即使按照世界纪录的标准，这也为人类的表现设定了很高的标准。现年 28 岁的加列耶夫已经在蒙眼国际象棋的稀有世界中脱颖而出。他喜欢鲜艳的衣服和不寻常的发型，并从 BASE 跳伞这项冒险运动中获得乐趣。他已经证明了自己也是一名强大的棋手。2013 年，在一场长达 10 小时的国际象棋马拉松比赛中，加列耶夫在头脑中同时下了 33 盘棋。他赢了 29 盘，一盘未输。这项技能已成为他的品牌：他称自己为 “蒙眼国王”。[br/]
[b]B.[/b]但是，加列耶夫的能力引起了国际象棋界以外的兴趣。加州大学洛杉矶分校（UCLA）的研究人员希望了解他和其他像他一样的人是如何完成这样的智力壮举的。现在他们有了第一批结果。加州大学洛杉矶分校记忆实验室负责人杰西-里斯曼（Jesse Rissman）说："对于大多数有成就的棋手来说，闭着眼睛下一盘棋的能力并不遥远。但铁木尔和其他一些人的非凡之处在于，他们能同时保持活跃的棋局数量。在我看来，这简直令人吃惊。[br/] [b]C.[/b]加列耶夫六岁时在家乡乌兹别克斯坦学习下棋。在祖父的指导下，他在八岁时第一次参加比赛，并很快迷上了比赛。16 岁时，他成为亚洲有史以来最年轻的国际象棋大师。不久后，他移居美国，并在学生时代帮助所在大学赢得了首个全国国际象棋锦标赛。2013 年，加列耶夫被评为美国最佳棋手第三名。[br/] 
[b]D.[/b]对不了解的人来说，蒙眼国际象棋似乎需要超人的技巧。但是，这种特技的展示可以追溯到几个世纪以前。欧洲有记载的第一盘棋是在 13 世纪的佛罗伦萨进行的。1947 年，阿根廷特级大师米格尔-纳伊多夫（Miguel Najdorf）在脑海中同时下了 45 盘棋，在 24 小时内赢了 39 盘。[br/]
[b]E.[/b]棋艺高超的棋手甚至会在不知不觉中掌握盲棋技巧。下棋的本质是在脑海中演算可能的棋步，看看它们是如何走出来的。由此，普通棋手会对棋子的布局、防御和攻击形成记忆。加列耶夫说："你会在脑海中重现棋局。很多球员都能做到我现在这样。真正的智力挑战来自于同时在头脑中进行多盘棋。不仅要记住每块棋盘上每颗棋子的位置，还必须在需要时忠实地回忆起这些位置，并根据每位棋手的棋步进行更新，然后再次可靠地存储起来，以便大脑能继续下一盘棋。第一步棋可能很难记住，因为它们相当无趣。但棋局结束时也会让人疲惫不堪。当加列耶夫疲惫时，他的记忆会变得模糊不清。有时，他只能凭借对棋子位置的零碎记忆下棋。[br/]
[b]F.[/b]科学家们首先让加列耶夫进行了一些标准的记忆测试。这些测试评估了他记忆数字、图片和文字的能力。其中一个经典测试是测量一个人在听到数字后能正反重复多少个数字。大多数人大约能复述七个数字。里斯曼说："他在这些标准测试中都没有出类拔萃的表现。'除了下棋，我们没有发现他似乎在其他方面有超常的天赋。'但接下来是脑部扫描。加列耶夫躺在机器里，里斯曼查看了棋手大脑各区域的连接情况。虽然结果还只是初步的，尚未公布，但扫描发现，加列耶夫大脑各部分之间的交流远远超过了平均水平，而这些部分构成了所谓的前顶叶控制网络。在与加列耶夫同时接受扫描的 63 人中，只有一两个人的得分更高。在几乎所有复杂的任务中，你都会用到这个网络。它能帮助你分配注意力，牢记规则，并确定是否应该做出反应，"里斯曼说。[br/]
[b]G.[/b]这并不是加列耶夫大脑中唯一的特殊暗示。扫描结果还表明，加列耶夫的视觉网络与其他大脑区域的联系比平常更加紧密。初步结果表明，他大脑中处理视觉图像（如棋盘）的区域可能与其他脑区有更强的联系，因此比正常人更强大。虽然分析结果尚未最终确定，但它们可能是加列耶夫非凡能力的第一批线索。[br/]
[b]H.[/b]为了打破世界纪录，加列耶夫希望在大约16小时内同时进行47次蒙眼游戏。他需要赢得 80% 的胜利才能夺冠。他说："我并不太在意胜率，这对我来说从来都不是问题。对我来说，蒙眼棋最重要的部分是我找到了一件可以全身心投入的事情。我很怀念这种痴迷的感觉。[br/]

He has a fondness for bright clothes and unusual hairstyles, and he gets his kicks from the adventure sport of BASE jumping. He has already proved himself a strong chess player, too. In a 10-hour chess marathon in 2013, Gareyev played 33 games in his head simultaneously. He won 29 and lost none. The skill has become his brand: he calls himself the Blindfold King. 



bBut Gareyev's prowess has drawn interest from beyond the chess-playing community. In the hope of understanding how he and others like him can perform such mental feats, researchers at the University of California in Los Angeles (UCLA) called him in for tests. They now have their first results. 'The ability to play a game of chess with your eyes closed is not a far reach for most accomplished players,' said Jesse Rissman, who runs a memory lab at UCLA. 'But the thing that's so remarkable about Timur and a few other individuals is the number of games they can keep active at once. To me it is simply astonishing.'

At 16, he was crowned Asia's youngest ever chess grandmaster. He moved to the US soon after, and as a student helped his university win its first national chess championship. In 2013, Gareyev was ranked the third best chess player in the US. 



dTo the uninitiated, blindfold chess seems to call for superhuman skill. But displays of the feat go back centuries. The first recorded game in Europe was played in 13th-century Florence. In 1947, the Argentinian grandmaster Miguel Najdorf played 45 simultaneous games in his mind, winning 39 in the 24-hour session.

From this, regular players develop a memory for the patterns the pieces make, the defences and attacks. 'You recreate it in your mind,' said Gareyev. 'A lot of players are capable of doing what I'm doing.' The real mental challenge comes from playing multiple games at once in the head. Not only must the positions of each piece on every board be memorised, they must be recalled faithfully when needed, updated with each player's moves, and then reliably stored again, so the brain can move on to the next board. First moves can be tough to remember because they are fairly uninteresting. But the ends of games are taxing too, as exhaustion sets in. When Gareyev is tired, his recall can get patchy. He sometimes makes moves based on only a fragmented memory of the pieces' positions.

One classic test measures how many numbers a person can repeat, both forwards and backwards, soon after hearing them. Most people manage about seven. It helps you to allocate attention, keep rules in mind, and work out whether you should be responding or not,' said Rissman. 



gIt was not the only hint of something special in Gareyev's brain. The scans also suggest that Gareyev's visual network is more highly connected to other brain parts than usual. hFor the world record attempt, Gareyev hopes to play 47 blindfold games at once in about 16 hours. He will need to win 80% to claim the title. 'I don't worry too much about the winning percentage, that's never been an issue for me,' he said. 'The most important part of blindfold chess for me is that I have found the one thing that I can fully dedicate myself to. I miss having an obsession.'

officialExplain.explain: 如前文中已经多次提到的那样：在“段落匹配信息”这种类型的题目中，大概率会有诸如reference, example, account, mention这类词汇出现，它们表达的意思其实都只是“提到、说到”，属于可以直接忽略的“废话”词汇，考生切勿多思多虑、徒增理解难度。本题干从定位到解题都不算难，因为读懂题干句即可看出，本题的定位和解答要点必定是落在“早期案例”这个信息上，而D段从第二句话开始就明确说到这种对弈方式可以回溯到若干世纪之前，接下来更是给出了两个从前的蒙眼棋手的故事，考生应可比较轻松而坚定地锁定答案。
本题的解答对考生来说可能颇费周章，最主要的原因在于：全段中没有任何一句话可以直接对应题干的意思表达，而是娓娓道来、逐句推进地解释了要蒙上眼睛下棋都需要具备哪些能力和经历哪些步骤，也就是用实际描述来诠释了这种对弈方式的“outline”。因此，考生需要耐心地至少看完本段的一半甚至一大半内容，才能更有把握地确认答案。不过，由于本段中同时还包含了第32题的答案，所以考生的阅读精力倒也颇有回报。
本题可能主要考查的是考生的阅读耐心和快速理解每句话大意的能力，因为对应题干的这两句话隐藏在F段的中间位置，需要考生读到相应位置；而一旦真的来到了这两句话面前，句意是非常直白好懂的。第六句说“他在这些标准测试中的任何一项上都没有什么突出表现”，第七句换了个措辞，其实还是在说“他除了象棋这一项没在其它方面表现出天赋异禀”，这两句话内容加起来非常明确地提示了考生：答案在此。
如果考生敢于仅仅凭借B段开头第一句话里原词重现的、来自于题干中的interest一词就选择本段段号作为答案的话，在解答这道题时也会因为运气大好而做对。不过当然最稳妥的方法还是逐句读懂B段中的每一句话，在理解句意和段意的基础上不带任何侥幸成分地做对此题。

本题一来考查考生的基础词汇掌握情况：不认识题干中priority这个高频阅读核心词汇的考生，请移步前文“必背词汇”列表，提升基础语言能力；如果认识这个词汇，就不难看出它对应的就是文章原句中的“The most important part”这个部分。二来，本题还考查了考生的阅读耐心：唯一对应题干句意思的原文答案句位于全文最后一段的倒数第二句，虽然不难看懂意思，但却需要考生一路看到了这里才能更有把握地解题。克服了这两道不算困难的难关，本题的解答就会比较轻松。

本题从题干句本身到对应原文句的理解都不困难，且由于前文中考生为了解答第28题其实已经要顺序阅读E段中每句话的意思了，所以即使原文句中taxing的用法考生并不熟悉，其实也可以利用上一句话中说“开头部分很困难”、联系本句话中“结尾部分taxing too”这样的上下文，推断出“一局棋的最后部分应该也很困难”这个结论；再加上原文句中还有明确表示“原因”的as一词的出现，因此考生应可比较稳妥地得出本题的答案。

本题的定位不难，因为全文开篇第一句就说到了即将展开的象棋对弈，考生只需再向下文阅读寻找选手情况，即可在第三句话里看到表达对比情况的信息：所有的对手都如常下棋，只有Gareyev自己会蒙上眼睛。即使在此处想杠一下“normal”这个词本身并没有提到是否蒙上眼睛，也可以通过全句使用了while表示不同和对比关系的上下文，推测出其他对手们肯定是没有蒙眼的，这与题干信息形成矛盾抵触，正确答案应为：FALSE。

本题的定位不算太难，因为“BASE jumping”这个信息原文出现在了A段里且距离上一道题位置并不算远。但原文里只说了Gareyev从这种运动中获得了极大的乐趣，并没有提及他是否在这个领域里也参加过比赛并获胜。不熟悉get sb’s kicks这个固定搭配用法的考生也许会由于不能确定它的意思而感到迟疑不绝(本文读者可移步前文“必背词汇”列表进行学习巩固)，这算是本题考查到的一个语言知识难点。一旦掌握了这个词组知识，就能看出“获得乐趣”与“赢得比赛”并不相关，而接下来向下文阅读几句、直到全段结束时，都再未提及这项运动的只言片语，可知答案应为：NOT GIVEN。
本题其实也不难定位，因为UCLA这个大写信息非常好找；然而原文中提及这所大学的时候只是作为对研究团队身份背景的介绍信息，完全没有给出“是否由这所大学率先开展了这个领域内研究”的任何相关内容，且直到下一题的信息已经出现也仍然如此，可以判定本题的正确答案也应为： NOT GIVEN。
本题虽然不像前两道题那样包含显眼的大写信息，但无论是题干句本身，还是对应的原文句，句式结构都不复杂，也没有使用什么困难生僻的词汇，考生应可比较轻松地看出题干中good与原文中accomplished两词之间的同义替换对应关系，继而得出正确答案应为：TRUE。
如果抛开答案出处位于全文比较靠后位置的F段这个因素，本题的定位可谓非常之友好。因为题干中的started by与相应原文中的first都是再基础不过的词汇了，考生只要足够耐心仔细地看到这一句，就能不费吹灰之力地看懂这一句，进而锁定本题答案应为：memory。

本题的解答难度也不高。首先，定位接近上一道题；其次，题干的理解难度适中。假如考生认识reverse这个词，就能同时回原文中寻找“正序”和“反序”这两个信息来定位；即使考生暂时还不认识这个单词，其实也可以根据题干中in reverse order与前面的in order形成的并列“and”关系，借以在原文中寻找“正序和另外某种不同于正序的order”这个信息。无论哪种情况，都能很快在正文中遇到forwards and backwards这个对应信息，进而判断出正确答案应为：numbers。

本题对于扎实推进平行阅读、逐句耐心看懂句意的考生会更为友好，因为题干中的“非比寻常量”这个信息在倒数第四句里的much greater than average这个表述中就已经有了明确的对应，进而对比寻找后也就可以得知答案应为：communication；然而如果把关注的重点放在directing attention这个限定说明上，则会在全段的最后一句话里的to allocate attention中才能得到最终确认。假如考生是在顺序平行阅读，那么读到此处相当于双重确认，自然也对自己的答案更加笃定。但如果反之，考生一开始若首先追求找到题干关键词的位置，则有可能在粗略浏览定位的过程中，先看到最后一句话里的题干原词attention，但却无法在这句话中找到相应答案，造成阅读时间精力的耗费。

本题的难度相对比较高。首先，是答案的定位处距离前几道题在原文中的位置可能略远，因为来自于下一个段落。不过其实在G段开头第一句，就有比较明确的信号告诉考生：从这里开始将会出现另一个讲述Gareyev大脑与众不同之处的信息。本题之所以略难的另一个原因在于对考生的灵活理解词汇能力的考查力度比较高。对应句中的process一词，在此句中采用的是相对较不常见的动词性用法“加工、处理”而非更为司空见惯的名词性用法“过程”；另外，题干中的input在对应原文中也采用了较为曲折隐晦的同义表达方式，因为如果从字典释义的角度来看，input是绝对没法和image进行直接同义替换的，考生需准确辨清词义、根据整个句子的完整意思表达，理解到“处理视觉图像”就是在“deal with某些类型的input”，继而得出正确答案应为：visual。
